html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 子不语怪 - A phrase from the *Analects* of Confucius, meaning 'Confucius does not speak of strange things.' Here, it is used ironically to contrast with the author's own approach.
  • 悖矣，然而意不悖也 - A paradoxical statement meaning 'it seems contradictory, but the intention is not contradictory.' It reflects the author's rationale for writing about strange events.
  • 天地至广大也，万物至纷赜也 - Literal phrases meaning 'the heavens and earth are extremely vast, and all things are extremely complex.' These set the philosophical context for the discussion.
  • 有其事必有其理，理之所在，怪何有焉 - A philosophical argument meaning 'there must be a reason for every event, and where there is a reason, there is no strangeness.' This reflects the author's belief in rational explanations for seemingly supernatural phenomena.
  • 圣人穷尽天地万物之理 - A phrase meaning 'sages exhaustively understand the principles of heaven, earth, and all things.' This highlights the sage's comprehensive knowledge.
  • 人见以为怪者，视之若寻常也 - A phrase meaning 'what people see as strange, sages regard as ordinary.' This emphasizes the sage's superior perspective.
  • 凤鸟河图，商羊萍实 - References to mythical creatures and symbols (phoenix, river map, Shangyang, and floating fruit) that are considered auspicious signs in Chinese culture.
  • 世人于目所未见，耳所未闻，一旦见之闻之，鲜不为怪者 - A phrase meaning 'when people see or hear something they have never seen or heard before, they are rarely not surprised.' This reflects the common human reaction to the unknown.
  • 少所见而多所怪 - A phrase meaning 'seeing little leads to being surprised by much.' This summarizes the idea that limited experience leads to frequent surprise.
  • 夜谭随录 - The title of the text, which can be translated as 'Night Talks and Casual Records.' It suggests a collection of stories told at night, possibly with a casual or informal tone.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 子不语怪 - A reference to Confucius, emphasizing the contrast between traditional Confucian values and the author's approach.
  • 凤鸟河图，商羊萍实 - These are well-known symbols in Chinese mythology and literature, often associated with auspicious signs and divine messages.
  • 夜谭随录 - The title itself alludes to a collection of stories or anecdotes, possibly told in a relaxed setting, reflecting the genre of the text.

Proper Nouns

  • 夜谭随录 - The title of the text, which can be translated as 'Night Talks and Casual Records.'
  • 闲斋氏 - The pseudonym of the author, which can be translated as 'Mr. Xianzai' or 'Mr. Leisurely Study.'

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 苟不以理穷 - Conditional phrase meaning 'if not by reason or logic.'
  • 人生世间 - Common phrase meaning 'living in the world.'
  • 无论天地万物广大纷赜 - Phrase meaning 'regardless of the vastness and complexity of the universe and all things.'
  • 即一身之耳目口鼻，言笑动止，死生梦幻 - List of bodily and existential phenomena, meaning 'even the eyes, ears, nose, mouth, speech, laughter, movement, stillness, life, death, dreams, and illusions of one's own body.'
  • 何者非怪 - Rhetorical question meaning 'which is not strange?'
  • 不求其理，而以见闻所不及者为怪，悖也 - Statement meaning 'not seeking the reason, but regarding what is beyond one's knowledge as strange, is absurd.'
  • 既求其理，而犹以见闻所不及者为怪，悖之甚者也 - Statement meaning 'even after seeking the reason, still regarding what is beyond one's knowledge as strange, is extremely absurd.'
  • 予今年四十有四矣 - Phrase meaning 'I am now forty-four years old.'
  • 未尝遇怪 - Phrase meaning 'I have never encountered anything strange.'
  • 每喜与二三酒朋 - Phrase meaning 'I often enjoy being with a few drinking companions.'
  • 于酒觞茶榻间 - Phrase meaning 'among wine cups, tea tables, and couches.'
  • 灭烛谈鬼，坐月说狐 - Phrase meaning 'talking about ghosts with candles extinguished, and discussing foxes under the moon.'
  • 稍涉匪夷 - Phrase meaning 'slightly involving the extraordinary or unusual.'
  • 辄为记载 - Phrase meaning 'immediately recording it.'
  • 日久成帙 - Phrase meaning 'over time, it has become a collection.'
  • 聊以自娱 - Phrase meaning 'just to amuse myself.'
  • 昔坡公强人说鬼 - Phrase meaning 'formerly, Su Dongpo forced people to talk about ghosts.'
  • 岂曰用广见闻 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is it to broaden one's knowledge?'
  • 抑曰谈虚无胜于言时事也 - Rhetorical question meaning 'or is it to say that discussing the void is better than talking about current affairs?'
  • 故人不妨妄言 - Phrase meaning 'therefore, one should not hesitate to speak foolishly.'
  • 己亦不妨妄听 - Phrase meaning 'one should also not hesitate to listen foolishly.'
  • 可妄言也，可妄听也，而独不可妄录哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'one can speak foolishly, one can listen foolishly, but why can one not record foolishly?'
  • 虽然妄言妄听而即妄录之 - Phrase meaning 'even so, to record foolishly what one speaks and listens to foolishly.'
  • 是亦怪也 - Phrase meaning 'this is also strange.'
  • 即《夜谭随录》，所谓为志怪之书也可 - Phrase meaning 'it is like *Yetai Suilu*, which is also called a book of strange tales.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 坡公 - Refers to Su Dongpo (Su Shi), a famous Song Dynasty poet and statesman known for his wit and literary talent.
  • 《夜谭随录》 - Refers to *Yetai Suilu*, a collection of strange tales by Ji Yun, a Qing Dynasty writer.

Proper Nouns

  • 坡公 - Su Dongpo (Su Shi).
  • 《夜谭随录》 - *Yetai Suilu* by Ji Yun.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乾隆辛亥夏六月 - Specific date in the Qing Dynasty, corresponding to June 1791.
  • 挥霍不吝 - Spending lavishly without hesitation.
  • 车马辐辏，门庭如市 - Vivid metaphor describing a bustling scene of many carriages and horses gathering at the entrance, likening it to a marketplace.
  • 虽齐之孟尝，赵之平原不是过也 - Comparison to two famous historical figures, Mengchang Jun of Qi and Pingyuan Jun of Zhao, both known for their hospitality and patronage of scholars.
  • 投一刺 - Submitted a business card or introduced himself with a formal note.
  • 刘接见，询其邦族 - Liu received him and asked about his family and origin.
  • 山东临朐秀才也 - A scholar who has passed the imperial examination but has not yet achieved higher degrees, from Linqu County, Shandong Province.
  • 游都门二十年矣 - Has been traveling in the capital for twenty years.
  • 闻公喜接纳，来作食客耳 - Heard that you enjoy welcoming guests, came to become a guest.
  • 刘大悦，与之往来 - Liu was very pleased, interacted with him frequently.
  • 亦时济其薪水 - Also sometimes helped with his expenses.
  • 崔率十余日一至 - Cui usually came every ten or more days.
  • 至必有所借贷 - Whenever he came, he would always borrow something.
  • 家人悉厌贱之 - The family members all disliked and looked down on him.
  • 刘独不以为琐 - Liu alone did not consider it trivial.
  • 每如其愿，未尝拂逆 - Always fulfilled his wishes and never refused him.
  • 如是者二年余 - Like this for over two years.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乾隆 - The reign title of Emperor Qianlong of the Qing Dynasty.
  • 辛亥 - A term in the Chinese stem-branch calendar system, corresponding to the year 1791.
  • 霁园 - A garden or residence, possibly a private retreat or study.
  • 蛾术斋 - A study or room named 'E Shu Study,' likely a place for scholarly activities.
  • 奉天 - An old name for Shenyang, the capital of Liaoning Province during the Qing Dynasty.
  • 先达 - Refers to respected scholars or elders.
  • 刘公 - A respected scholar named Liu.
  • 孟尝 - Mengchang Jun, a historical figure known for his hospitality and patronage of scholars.
  • 平原 - Pingyuan Jun, another historical figure known for his generosity and support of intellectuals.
  • 山东临朐 - A place name, referring to Linqu County in Shandong Province.
  • 秀才 - A scholar who has passed the imperial examination but has not yet achieved higher degrees.
  • 都门 - Refers to the capital city, likely Beijing.
  • 崔元素 - The name of a person who introduces himself to Liu.

Proper Nouns

  • 乾隆辛亥夏六月 - June 1791.
  • 霁园主人 - The author or proprietor of the 'Ji Garden.'
  • 蛾术斋 - The 'E Shu Study.'
  • 奉天 - Shenyang.
  • 刘公 - Liu, a respected scholar.
  • 孟尝 - Mengchang Jun.
  • 平原 - Pingyuan Jun.
  • 山东临朐 - Linqu County, Shandong Province.
  • 崔元素 - Cui Yuansu, a scholar.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遭大故 - Literally 'encountering a great calamity,' referring to a significant personal tragedy.
  • 资产荡尽 - Means 'all assets were wiped out,' indicating complete financial ruin.
  • 一贫如洗 - A fixed expression meaning 'as poor as a church mouse' or 'utterly destitute.'
  • 屡试不第 - Means 'failed the exams repeatedly,' referring to unsuccessful attempts at the imperial examinations.
  • 亲故白眼相向 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'received disdainful looks from relatives and friends.'
  • 动辄得咎 - Means 'to be blamed for anything one does,' indicating constant criticism.
  • 传为口实 - Means 'became a subject of ridicule or criticism,' suggesting that one's situation was a topic of gossip.
  • 渐至不相闻问 - Means 'gradually came to be ignored,' indicating a decline in social relations.
  • 婢仆逃散 - Means 'maids and servants fled,' describing the loss of household staff.
  • 心作罪以求去者接踵 - Means 'those who sought to leave by fabricating offenses followed one after another,' indicating a series of dishonest departures.
  • 仅存一老仆 - Means 'only one old servant remained,' emphasizing the loneliness and reduced circumstances.
  • 内则一妻一女一子，鼎足而三焉 - Means 'inside there were one wife, one daughter, and one son, making three in total,' using a metaphor to describe the family structure.
  • 会腊尽，牛衣尘甑，无以卒岁 - Means 'when the year was nearly over, they had nothing but coarse clothes and a dusty pot, unable to make it through the year,' describing extreme poverty.
  • 女能诗 - Means 'the daughter could write poetry,' indicating her literary talent.
  • 戏吟曰 - Means 'playfully recited,' suggesting a light-hearted tone despite the dire circumstances.
  • 闷杀连朝雨雪天 - Means 'the continuous rain and snow days are so depressing,' expressing frustration with the weather.
  • 教人何处觅黄棉 - Means 'how can one find yellow cotton,' a metaphor for warmth and comfort.
  • 岁除不比逢寒时 - Means 'New Year's Eve is not the same as a cold day,' contrasting the festive occasion with the harsh reality.
  • 底事厨中也禁烟 - Means 'why is there no smoke in the kitchen,' indicating the lack of food preparation.
  • 此际玉搂起粟 - Means 'at this moment, the jade bed rises with grains,' a metaphor for the cold weather making the bed feel like it's rising with grains.
  • 若可煮食，足够一饱 - Means 'if it could be cooked, it would be enough for a meal,' a humorous suggestion given the dire circumstances.
  • 今年犹戴昔年天 - Means 'this year still wears the sky of last year,' suggesting a continuation of hardship.
  • 昔日轻裘今破棉 - Means 'the light fur of the past is now patched with cotton,' indicating a decline in living standards.
  • 寄语东风休报信 - Means 'send a message to the east wind not to report,' a metaphor for not wanting to hear bad news.
  • 春来无力出厨烟 - Means 'when spring comes, there is no strength to produce smoke in the kitchen,' indicating the continued poverty and lack of resources.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刘迭 - A fictional or historical figure, likely a scholar or official who faced significant personal and financial difficulties.
  • 亲故 - Refers to relatives and close friends.
  • 屡试不第 - Refers to the imperial examinations, a crucial part of the Chinese civil service system.
  • 婢仆 - Refers to female servants and male servants, indicating the household staff.
  • 牛衣尘甑 - Metaphorical expressions for coarse clothing and a dusty pot, symbolizing poverty.
  • 岁除 - Refers to the last day of the lunar year, a time of reflection and preparation for the new year.
  • 玉搂起粟 - A metaphor for the cold weather making the bed feel like it's rising with grains.
  • 东风 - Refers to the east wind, often associated with the arrival of spring.
  • 春来无力出厨烟 - A metaphor for the continued poverty and lack of resources even in the spring.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘迭 - A fictional or historical figure.
  • 岁除 - The last day of the lunar year.
  • 东风 - The east wind, often associated with the arrival of spring.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 怒之以目 - Classical expression meaning 'to glare at someone,' indicating anger or disapproval through facial expression.
  • 啜汁者岂止一人 - Phrase meaning 'those who have tasted the juice are not just one person,' likely referring to multiple people who have benefited or been involved in a situation.
  • 年近岁逼 - Phrase meaning 'as the year approaches and the season presses,' indicating the urgency of time, especially in the context of approaching winter or the end of the year.
  • 推敲丑态 - Phrase meaning 'the awkward and indecisive manner of deliberation,' often used to describe someone hesitating or struggling to make a decision.
  • 韭露挽歌 - Metaphorical expression referring to a lament or dirge, possibly alluding to a poem or song about the fleeting nature of life, like the short-lived life of chives (韭菜) and their dew.
  • 微汝言，吾几忘之矣 - Phrase meaning 'without your words, I would have almost forgotten,' indicating that the speaker was reminded of something by the other person.
  • 赤手回 - Phrase meaning 'returning empty-handed,' indicating failure or lack of success in an attempt.
  • 名节扫地尽矣 - Phrase meaning 'one's reputation and integrity are completely ruined,' indicating a severe loss of honor or standing.
  • 满拟必获如意 - Phrase meaning 'fully expecting to achieve one's desires,' indicating high hopes or expectations.
  • 骤闻此变 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly hearing this change,' indicating a sudden and unexpected turn of events.
  • 不禁索然 - Phrase meaning 'unable to help feeling disappointed or disheartened,' indicating a sense of loss or discouragement.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 妻怒之以目 - The passage describes a wife expressing her displeasure through a glare, which is a common expression of anger in Chinese culture.
  • 良朋密友 - Refers to close friends or trusted companions, indicating a relationship of mutual support and trust.
  • 吃着俱无 - Refers to a state of poverty or lack of resources, where even basic necessities are scarce.
  • 儿女子 - Refers to young people or children, often used in a derogatory sense to imply immaturity or lack of responsibility.
  • 朱知县 - Refers to a magistrate named Zhu, a local official who holds a position of authority in the community.
  • 丁艰在家 - Refers to a period of mourning for a family member, during which the person is expected to refrain from social activities and official duties.
  • 宦囊颇厚 - Refers to the substantial salary or income that an official receives from their position, indicating wealth and status.
  • 走一简 - Refers to writing a letter, a common form of communication in classical Chinese society.
  • 聊济燃眉 - Refers to providing temporary relief or assistance, often in a time of urgent need.
  • 名节扫地尽矣 - Refers to the loss of one's reputation and moral standing, a serious concern in traditional Chinese culture.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘 - The name of the husband, Liu.
  • 朱知县 - The name of the magistrate, Zhu.
  • 顺城门 - The name of a gate in the city wall, likely referring to a specific location in the city.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 莫逆交 - A term for the closest and most intimate friendship, where there is no disagreement or conflict.
  • 总角之交 - Refers to childhood friends, as children tie their hair in two buns (总角) before reaching adulthood.
  • 泛泛 - Means superficial or casual; in this context, it suggests a lack of depth or sincerity.
  • 面朋口友 - A derogatory term for superficial friends who are only friendly on the surface.
  • 通财之义 - Refers to the principle of sharing wealth or resources, often associated with deep friendship.
  • 道义之交 - A friendship based on shared principles or moral values, rather than material interests.
  • 抚髀叹 - A gesture of deep reflection or sorrow, often accompanied by a sigh.
  • 挑灯作札 - The act of writing a letter by candlelight, symbolizing earnest effort.
  • 罄吐肝膈 - Means to express one's deepest thoughts or feelings fully and honestly.
  • 付老仆持送 - To entrust a letter to an old servant for delivery.
  • 世胄阀阅 - Refers to a family of noble lineage with a long history of high social status.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 莫逆交 - A concept from classical Chinese literature emphasizing the highest level of friendship.
  • 总角之交 - A term rooted in ancient Chinese customs, where children's appearance and behavior were significant.
  • 面朋口友 - A term that reflects the value placed on genuine relationships in Confucian ethics.
  • 通财之义 - A principle that aligns with Confucian ideals of mutual support and trust in friendships.
  • 道义之交 - A concept that emphasizes the importance of moral and ethical foundations in relationships.
  • 挑灯作札 - A literary and cultural practice that symbolizes dedication and sincerity in communication.
  • 世胄阀阅 - A term that highlights the significance of family background and social status in traditional Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 妻 - The wife of the speaker.
  • 刘 - The speaker's name.
  • 杨君 - A friend from childhood.
  • 靳公子 - A nobleman from the south of the city.
  • 南城 - The southern part of the city.
  • 畿辅 - The surrounding areas of the capital, indicating a wide landholding.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公子与刘为世交，又属至戚 - Long-standing friendship and close relatives.
  • 夜以继日 - Day and night, emphasizing intensity and continuity.
  • 所讲论非忠义大节，即出世大道 - Discussions about loyalty, righteousness, and transcendence.
  • 互相诱掖 - Encourage and support each other.
  • 不啻同胞 - Not unlike brothers, deep bond.
  • 立脚不随流俗 - Stand firm, not follow the crowd.
  • 留心学做古人 - Pay attention to learning from the ancients.
  • 阅札即刻复答 - Respond immediately to letters.
  • 叨在知己 - Fortunate to have a true friend.
  • 亟当如命 - Urgently follow the command.
  • 心与力违 - Heart and strength are at odds.
  • 束手无策 - At a loss, unable to do anything.
  • 君但勉为尚志之士 - Strive to be a person of high ideals.
  • 无自暴弃 - Do not give up on yourself.
  • 又何忧贫贱哉 - Why worry about poverty and low status.
  • 天生刘君，必非碌碌者 - God has created Mr. Liu, he will not be ordinary.
  • 君姑待之 - Wait for it.
  • 保有大富贵日也 - Day of great wealth and honor.
  • 好义如弟者 - Values righteousness like a brother.
  • 值此危急之秋 - Critical time.
  • 竟坐视良朋之困 - Sit idly by, watch friend in trouble.
  • 不能一援手救 - Unable to lend a helping hand.
  • 殊堪自愧 - Truly shameful.
  • 唯知己者谅之耳 - Only true friends can understand.
  • 忿，掷书于地曰 - Angry, threw letter on ground.
  • 嗬嗬 - Interjection, frustration or anger.
  • 平日披肝胆，谈道德 - Share hearts, discuss morality.
  • 何啻羊、左、任、黎 - Not unlike Yang, Zuo, Ren, Li.
  • 每举一子一女，犹以百金为寿 - Offer hundred gold coins as gift.
  • 急切相需，乃不破一文 - Urgent need, not spend a single coin.
  • 反以肤词迂说相敦勉 - Use superficial words to encourage.
  • 所谓道义之交，固如是乎 - Is this what is called a friendship based on righteousness?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 世交 - Long-standing friendship across generations.
  • 至戚 - Close relatives, like siblings.
  • 忠义大节 - Loyalty, righteousness, great principles.
  • 出世大道 - Path to transcendence and enlightenment.
  • 诱掖 - Encouragement and support.
  • 立脚不随流俗 - Stand firm, not follow the crowd.
  • 留心学做古人 - Pay attention to learning from the ancients.
  • 阅札即刻复答 - Respond immediately to letters.
  • 叨在知己 - Fortunate to have a true friend.
  • 亟当如命 - Urgently follow the command.
  • 心与力违 - Heart and strength are at odds.
  • 束手无策 - At a loss, unable to do anything.
  • 尚志之士 - Person of high ideals.
  • 无自暴弃 - Do not give up on yourself.
  • 贫贱 - Poverty and low status.
  • 天生刘君，必非碌碌者 - God has created Mr. Liu, he will not be ordinary.
  • 君姑待之 - Wait for it.
  • 大富贵 - Great wealth and honor.
  • 好义如弟者 - Values righteousness like a brother.
  • 值此危急之秋 - Critical time.
  • 竟坐视良朋之困 - Sit idly by, watch friend in trouble.
  • 不能一援手救 - Unable to lend a helping hand.
  • 殊堪自愧 - Truly shameful.
  • 唯知己者谅之耳 - Only true friends can understand.
  • 忿，掷书于地曰 - Angry, threw letter on ground.
  • 嗬嗬 - Interjection, frustration or anger.
  • 平日披肝胆，谈道德 - Share hearts, discuss morality.
  • 何啻羊、左、任、黎 - Not unlike Yang, Zuo, Ren, Li.
  • 每举一子一女，犹以百金为寿 - Offer hundred gold coins as gift.
  • 急切相需，乃不破一文 - Urgent need, not spend a single coin.
  • 反以肤词迂说相敦勉 - Use superficial words to encourage.
  • 所谓道义之交，固如是乎 - Is this what is called a friendship based on righteousness?

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大概未曾交得一人 - Indicates that the person has not made many friends, suggesting a lack of social connections.
  • 盍拚一失色 - Suggests trying or attempting to change one's appearance or demeanor.
  • 与之通融 - Means to accommodate or make allowances for them.
  • 尚三呼不应 - Indicates that despite repeated attempts, there is no response, suggesting a lack of reciprocation or acknowledgment.
  • 琐琐姻娅 - Implies that these relatives are not significant or important.
  • 又何望乎 - Means 'what can one expect from them?'
  • 闻门有剥啄声 - Describes the sound of someone knocking on the door.
  • 彼尚欲来刲瘦胫耶 - A sarcastic remark suggesting that someone would come to cut off thin legs.
  • 焉知并胫也无 - Means 'how do we know they even have legs?'
  • 正恐无下刀处 - Suggests that there is no place to cut, implying that the situation is dire.
  • 不然 - Means 'not so' or 'it is not the case.'
  • 此空谷足音也 - An idiom meaning a rare and valuable visitor, like the sound of footsteps in an empty valley.
  • 延之入 - Means to invite or allow someone to enter.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五伦 - Refers to the five relationships in Confucian ethics: ruler-subject, father-son, husband-wife, elder brother-younger brother, and friend-friend.
  • 秀才 - A title given to those who have passed the imperial examinations but have not yet been appointed to official positions.
  • 空谷足音 - An idiom meaning a rare and valuable visitor, like the sound of footsteps in an empty valley.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘 - The name of the main character.
  • 崔秀才 - The name of the visitor, who is a scholar who has passed the imperial examinations.

[9]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 劉君縱理不入於口 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'Mr. Liu, though you may not speak eloquently,' where '縱理不入於口' suggests that his speech or reasoning might not be persuasive or articulate.
  • 鼯技易窮 - Metaphorical expression using the weasel (鼯) to suggest that skills or abilities can easily be exhausted or fail.
  • 青松落色 - Metaphorical expression suggesting the loss of vitality or the fading of one's former glory, often associated with aging or decline.
  • 槿心朝在，夕不存矣 - Metaphorical expression using the hibiscus (槿) to illustrate the fleeting nature of life, where '朝在' means 'present in the morning' and '夕不存矣' means 'not exist in the evening.'
  • 束手待毙 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to surrender or give up hope,' literally 'to tie one's hands and wait to die.'
  • 負重涉遠，不择地而休 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'when carrying a heavy load and traveling a long distance, one does not choose a place to rest,' suggesting perseverance and determination.
  • 累重家贫，不择禄而仕 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'when burdened with heavy responsibilities and living in poverty, one does not choose a salary and goes to work,' suggesting a willingness to take on difficult tasks regardless of personal gain.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 盟车笠，订金兰 - Refers to the ancient Chinese tradition of forming deep friendships, where '盟车笠' (alliance under a carriage and hat) and '订金兰' (binding a friendship like the metal and wood of a sword and scabbard) symbolize strong and enduring bonds.
  • 廉将军免官客去，翟廷尉复职客来 - Refers to the historical figures Liang Ji (廉将军) and Zhai Tingwei (翟廷尉). Liang Ji was a general who lost his position and had his guests leave, while Zhai Tingwei regained his position and had new guests arrive, illustrating the fickleness of human relationships.
  • 智者当务之为急 - Classical phrase meaning 'wise people prioritize urgent matters,' emphasizing the importance of focusing on immediate needs.

  • 聊博升斗 - Means 'to barely make a living,' referring to earning a meager livelihood.
  • 峣峣者易缺，皎皎者易污 - An idiom meaning 'The lofty are easily broken, and the pure are easily defiled,' suggesting that those who are too proud or pure are vulnerable.
  • 非所以自完也 - Means 'It is not a way to preserve oneself,' indicating that the previous suggestion is not advisable.
  • 局促效辕下驹 - A metaphor for being confined and acting like a horse tied to a post, symbolizing a lack of freedom or ambition.
  • 夙所羞也 - Means 'It has always been a source of shame,' indicating a long-standing aversion to such a life.
  • 奇货可居，垄断可登 - A metaphor for hoarding valuable goods and controlling the market, suggesting a business approach.
  • 贪贾三之，廉贾五之 - Means 'Greedy merchants triple their profit, while honest merchants quintuple it,' suggesting that honest business practices can lead to greater success.
  • 觊觎分毫，镏铢必较 - Means 'To covet every small amount and to be overly concerned with minor details,' indicating a miserly attitude.
  • 素所鄙夷，而弗屑者也 - Means 'It has always been something I despise and would not stoop to,' indicating a strong aversion to such behavior.
  • 然则度君之心，量君之志，欲更扬眉吐气，非官不能矣 - Means 'Therefore, considering your heart and aspirations, if you wish to rise above, it can only be through becoming an official,' suggesting that only through officialdom can one achieve true success.
  • 欲为官，须登第；欲登第，须理旧业读书；欲读书，须膏火之费 - Means 'To become an official, one must pass the imperial examinations; to pass the examinations, one must study the classics; to study, one must have the expenses for oil and candles,' outlining the necessary steps and costs involved.
  • 吾视君皆未易办也 - Means 'I see that all these things are not easy for you,' indicating that the speaker recognizes the challenges faced by the listener.
  • 吾有钱八十千，可辇至 - Means 'I have eighty thousand coins, which I can bring to you,' offering financial assistance.
  • 君方同病，讵忍波累 - Means 'You are also in the same situation; how could I add to your troubles?' indicating empathy and reluctance to impose additional burdens.
  • 人弃我取，人取我予，夫何辞焉 - Means 'When others discard, I take; when others take, I give; why should I hesitate?' suggesting a pragmatic and opportunistic approach.
  • 遂言别 - Means 'So they parted ways,' indicating the end of the conversation.
  • 移时，以车辇八十千至，刘大感谢，欲备一餐相款 - Means 'After some time, the money was brought in a cart; Liu greatly thanked him and wanted to prepare a meal as a gesture of gratitude,' indicating the arrival of the money and Liu's response.

  • 自述姓花，世系陇西，徙来顺天两世矣 - She introduced herself as the surname Flower, her ancestral lineage traces back to Longxi, and she has lived in Shuntian for two generations.
  • 宅后梁家园，儿家故居也 - Her home is behind Liang Garden, which was our family's former residence.
  • 与君有夙份，故相就耳 - I have had a predestined connection with you, hence I come to you.
  • 诚非偶然也 - It is indeed not by chance.
  • 第圣人之道，胞与为怀，故万物并育而不相害 - But the way of the sage, embracing all as one, allows all things to grow together without harming each other.
  • 予洞此理久矣 - I have understood this principle for a long time.
  • 卿狐耶鬼耶？幸勿见诳 - Are you a fox spirit or a ghost? Please do not deceive me.
  • 儿仙子也。奈何疑为狐鬼？ - I am a fairy. Why do you doubt that I am a fox spirit or a ghost?
  • 见卿饮食如凡人，且不戒荤酒，仙子固如是乎？ - I see you eating and drinking like an ordinary person, and you do not abstain from meat and alcohol. Are immortals really like this?
  • 人谓执而不化者为书痴，今信然矣 - People say that those who cling to the book without changing are bookworms. Now I believe it.
  • 君既以书致诘，即请以书解纷 - Since you have questioned me using the book, I will use the book to resolve the confusion.
  • 君独不见《神仙》诸记之所载乎？ - Have you not seen what is recorded in the various accounts of immortals?
  • 龙肝麟脯，惟仙食之 - Dragon liver and unicorn meat, only immortals eat them.
  • 玉醴金浆，惟仙饮之 - Jade liquor and golden wine, only immortals drink them.
  • 他如千年之桃、万年之藕、百石之醪、凤凰之髓、以及交梨火枣、橘液霞觞 - Other things like thousand-year-old peaches, ten-thousand-year-old lotus roots, hundred-stone wine, phoenix marrow, and various other exotic fruits and wines.
  • 凡此之属，散见于诗书者，指不胜屈 - All these things are scattered throughout the poems and books, too numerous to count.

  • 春盡則僵；蟬飲而不食，秋殘則枯；蜉蝣不飲不食，乃朝生而暮死 - Examples of creatures that die shortly after their life cycle ends, used to argue against the idea that not eating or drinking leads to immortality.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 校必多方散其侪伍 - The schoolmaster must scatter his companions in various ways.
  • 独扶筇至角楼下 - He alone supports a walking stick to the corner of the building.
  • 告曰：“致语三姑娘，我今日上班矣。” - He said, 'Convey to Miss San, I am on duty today.'
  • 至晚女果至 - By evening, the girl indeed arrived.
  • 二婢随进酒馔，珍美错陈 - Two maids followed, bringing in wine and delicacies, beautifully arranged.
  • 校夜夜餍之 - The schoolmaster enjoyed it every night.
  • 每心有所欲，未发，女已先知，无不咄嗟立办 - Whenever he had a desire, before he expressed it, the girl already knew and could fulfill it instantly.
  • 校尝以玉环赠，女再拜以受，什袭藏之 - The schoolmaster once gave her a jade ring, and she bowed twice to receive it, carefully storing it away.
  • 校与语谈时，自念皤然一翁，将旦夕犯雾露，泣数行下 - When talking with her, he thought of himself as an old man, about to face the morning dew and mist, and tears streamed down his face.
  • 女曰：“勿伤，儿视爹尚可三十年活也。” - The girl said, 'Do not be sad, I believe Father can live for another thirty years.'
  • 乃授校以导引之术，行之颇效 - She then taught him the technique of guiding and leading, which proved effective.
  • 女无他异处，惟喜面，一夜恒四五次 - The girl had no other unusual behavior, only she enjoyed seeing him, doing so several times a night.
  • 校少子方娶，苦无杯盘，将赁诸市 - The schoolmaster's younger son was about to get married, but they lacked tableware, planning to rent it from the market.
  • 女曰：“是无庸，儿当为爹假之。” - The girl said, 'There is no need, I will borrow it for you.'
  • 至期，果有金银器物，杂然陈于房中，不测所自 - By the time, there were indeed silver and gold utensils, variously displayed in the room, their origin unknown.

  • 校次子为护军，闻女美，潜上城至值所，从窗隙窃窥，竟无所见，但翁一人自言自笑自饮而已 - The schoolmaster's second son, a guard, heard of the girl's beauty, secretly climbed the city wall to the duty post, peering through a window crack, but saw nothing, only the old man talking to himself, laughing, and drinking alone.

  • 作淫戏于墙阴 - 作淫戏 refers to engaging in sexual acts, and 墙阴 means 'in the shadow of the wall.' This phrase describes a private and illicit act.
  • 略不羞避 - 略不 means 'not at all,' and 羞避 means 'ashamed to avoid.' This phrase emphasizes the lack of shame or attempt to hide the act.
  • 张问曰 - 张 is likely a proper name, and 问曰 means 'asked.' This indicates that someone named Zhang is asking a question.
  • 彼女子即渠之爱姬翠华 - 彼女子 means 'that woman,' 即 means 'is,' 渠之 means 'his,' and 爱姬翠华 is the name of the woman, 'Cui Hua.' This phrase identifies the woman as the man's beloved concubine.
  • 彼男子即渠之娈童郑禄也 - 彼男子 means 'that man,' 即 means 'is,' 渠之 means 'his,' and 娈童郑禄 is the name of the man, 'Zheng Lu.' This phrase identifies the man as the man's male lover.
  • 因渠病卧，故私约于此 - 因 means 'because,' 渠病卧 means 'he was ill and lying down,' 故 means 'so,' and 私约于此 means 'they privately agreed to meet here.' This explains why they were meeting in secret.
  • 彼方自谓隐密，岂暇见我辈，又岂意我辈见之明且晰哉 - 彼方自谓隐密 means 'they thought it was secret,' 岂暇见我辈 means 'how could they spare the time to see us,' and 又岂意我辈见之明且晰哉 means 'how could they have expected us to see it clearly.' This emphasizes the characters' belief in their secrecy.
  • 张目知县而笑 - 张目 means 'looked at,' 知县 means 'the magistrate,' and 而笑 means 'and laughed.' This describes Zhang's reaction.
  • 知县亦俯首不语 - 知县 means 'the magistrate,' 亦 means 'also,' 俯首 means 'lowered his head,' and 不语 means 'did not speak.' This describes the magistrate's reaction.
  • 至水窦前，复见二人，结束同二役 - 至水窦前 means 'at the water drain,' 复见 means 'again saw,' 二人 means 'two people,' and 结束同二役 means 'dressed similarly to the two servants.' This describes another encounter.
  • 械一人，囚首面而立 - 械 means 'bound,' 一人 means 'one person,' 囚首 means 'with a prisoner's head,' and 面而立 means 'stood facing.' This describes the bound person.
  • 已拘得乎 - 已 means 'already,' 拘得 means 'captured,' and 乎 is a particle that can be translated as 'have we?' This is a question about whether the person has been captured.
  • 应曰 - 应 means 'answered,' and 曰 means 'said.' This indicates a response.
  • 见知县欲哭 - 见 means 'saw,' 知县 means 'the magistrate,' 欲 means 'wanted to,' and 哭 means 'cry.' This describes the person's reaction upon seeing the magistrate.
  • 役急批其颊而止 - 役 means 'servant,' 急 means 'quickly,' 批其颊 means 'slapped his cheek,' and 而止 means 'and stopped.' This describes the servant's action.
  • 张私诘此人为谁 - 张 is likely a proper name, 私诘 means 'privately asked,' and 此人为谁 means 'who this person is.' This describes Zhang's inquiry.
  • 即渠之幕宾，主刑名者郭某也 - 即 means 'is,' 渠之 means 'his,' 幕宾 means 'counselor,' 主刑名者 means 'who handles legal matters,' and 郭某 is the name of the person, 'Mr. Guo.' This identifies the person.
  • 与同案，故同拘耳 - 与同案 means 'in the same case,' 故 means 'so,' and 同拘耳 means 'they were detained together.' This explains why the person was detained.
  • 闻内宅哭声群起 - 闻 means 'heard,' 内宅 means 'inside the house,' 哭声 means 'crying sounds,' and 群起 means 'rose up together.' This describes the collective reaction.
  • 时至矣 - 时至矣 means 'the time has come.' This indicates that a significant moment has arrived.
  • 遂出至坊间，预有二人驻囚舆二辆相候于通衢 - 遂出 means 'then went out,' 至坊间 means 'to the marketplace,' 预有 means 'previously had,' 二人 means 'two people,' 驻囚舆二辆 means 'two prisoner carts,' and 相候于通衢 means 'waiting at the main road.' This describes the setup for the next scene.
  • 四役因纳知县与郭于舆中 - 四役 means 'four servants,' 因 means 'therefore,' 纳 means 'put,' 知县 means 'the magistrate,' 与 means 'and,' 郭 means 'Mr. Guo,' and 于舆中 means 'into the carts.' This describes the action of putting the magistrate and Mr. Guo into the carts.
  • 嘱张曰 - 嘱 means 'instructed,' 张 is likely a proper name, and 曰 means 'said.' This indicates a command or instruction.
  • 汝自归，慎勿泄于人也 - 汝 means 'you,' 自归 means 'return on your own,' 慎 means 'be careful,' 勿 means 'not to,' and 泄于人也 means 'reveal to others.' This is a warning to Zhang.
  • 言讫，超舆叱牛而去 - 言讫 means 'after finishing speaking,' 超舆 means 'jumped onto the carts,' 叱牛 means 'whipped the oxen,' and 而去 means 'and left.' This describes the final action.

  • 爱姬 - Refers to a beloved concubine, a term used to describe a favored mistress in a noble household.
  • 娈童 - Refers to a young male lover, often in a homosexual relationship, a term that reflects the social norms and practices of the time.
  • 幕宾 - Refers to a counselor or advisor, often a learned individual who assists in legal and administrative matters.
  • 刑名 - Refers to legal matters or the handling of legal cases, a specialized field of expertise.
  • 内宅 - Refers to the inner part of a house, typically the private living quarters, where women and family members reside.
  • 坊间 - Refers to the marketplace or public square, a central gathering place in traditional Chinese towns and cities.
  • 通衢 - Refers to a main road or thoroughfare, a central route in the town or city.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 闲斋曰 - A formal way to introduce a statement or opinion, similar to 'The Idle Studio says.'
  • 麝之见猎，以脐之有香也 - A metaphorical expression where the deer's reaction to the hunter is attributed to the musk's fragrance. '麝' (shè) refers to musk deer, '见猎' (jiàn liè) means 'to see hunting,' and '脐之有香' (qí zhī yǒu xiāng) means 'because of the fragrance of the musk gland.'
  • 象有齿，犀有角，鹖有尾，雕有翎，鲏有皮，□有膏，□有甲，螰有珠，貂有毛，蚺有胆，皆麝类也 - A series of metaphors comparing various animals and their distinctive features to the musk deer. Each animal is described by its most notable characteristic, suggesting that these animals are all like the musk deer in some way.
  • 彼方自以为天之笃之，而不知天之毒之也 - A philosophical statement indicating that these animals believe they are favored by heaven, but they do not realize that this favor is actually a form of poison or curse.
  • 惟人亦然，女有色，士有才 - A parallel structure suggesting that humans are also like the animals, with women having beauty and men having talent.
  • 兰岩曰 - Another formal introduction, similar to 'The Blue Rock says.'
  • 二獐以情死，以香败 - A metaphorical statement where two deer die because of their emotions and are defeated by their fragrance. '二獐' (èr zhāng) refers to two deer, '以情死' (yǐ qíng sǐ) means 'die because of their emotions,' and '以香败' (yǐ xiāng bài) means 'defeated by their fragrance.'
  • 倘能自守一时之欲，则古洞幽深，谁复得而扰之哉 - A conditional statement suggesting that if the deer could control their desires for a moment, the ancient cave would remain undisturbed. '倘能自守一时之欲' (tǎng néng zì shǒu yī shí zhī yù) means 'if they could control their desires for a moment,' and '则古洞幽深，谁复得而扰之哉' (zé gǔ dòng yōu shēn, shuí fù dé ér rǎo zhī zāi) means 'then the ancient cave would remain undisturbed.'
  • 甚矣！情欲一动即死机也，香气所闻即败兆也 - A strong statement emphasizing that emotional desire is a death trap and the fragrance is a sign of defeat. '甚矣' (shèn yǐ) means 'how great,' '情欲一动即死机也' (qíng yù yī dòng jí sǐ jī yě) means 'emotional desire is a death trap,' and '香气所闻即败兆也' (xiāng qì suǒ wén jí bài zhào yě) means 'the fragrance is a sign of defeat.'
  • 惜哉 - An expression of regret or pity.

  • 雄，俊美也 - A description of the person's appearance, meaning 'handsome and beautiful.'
  • 噶雄者何？人名也 - A question and answer format, meaning 'What is Gaxiong? It is a person's name.'
  • 人而名噶雄，以其人少且俊也 - An explanation of the name, meaning 'The person is named Gaxiong because he is young and handsome.'
  • 雄，杨姓，本粤东人，其祖为河州副将，卒于官，路远，柩不能归，葬河州 - A biography of the person, meaning 'Xiong, surnamed Yang, originally from Guangdong, his grandfather was a deputy general of Hezhou, died in office, the journey was too far, the coffin could not be returned, so he was buried in Hezhou.'
  • 遂家焉 - A continuation of the biography, meaning 'and settled there.'
  • 父锟为守备，四十而死 - Another part of the biography, meaning 'His father, Kun, was a guard commander, died at the age of forty.'
  • 雄幼孤，长养叔婶 - A continuation of the biography, meaning 'Xiong was orphaned at a young age and raised by his uncle and aunt.'
  • 叔为千总 - A description of the uncle, meaning 'His uncle was a hundred-man commander.'
  • 是时大同周公文锦，为河州副将，怜其宦裔，落拓，乃以雄为余丁，令掌书记 - A description of a significant event, meaning 'At that time, Zhou Gong Wenjin of Datong was the deputy general of Hezhou, pitied his official lineage, and was destitute, so he appointed Xiong as a surplus soldier and ordered him to manage the records.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The fox instructed the owner to buy several hundred taels of southern lead and insert it into a hidden passage, warning him not to spy. As a result, every night after three o'clock, he would immediately hear a sound of a bellows or wind instrument in the room, which would finally stop after five o'clock. After forty-nine days, the fox called the owner to the hidden passage and gave him silver. The fine threads or strands had all turned into patterned treasures. The owner was amazed and grateful, and he worshiped the fox devoutly. Six or seven people came and went to retrieve and store the treasures, and it took the whole night to finish. He weighed them and found they amounted to five thousand taels. He asked the fox where these things came from and if they could be preserved in the world. The fox said, 'I have a special connection with you, so I used a bit of magic to smelt and transform them for you. They are not fake, so why can't they be preserved? You may use them without any doubt, and I will also leave from here.' The owner earnestly tried to persuade him to stay, but he did not hear any response. After a while, it grew increasingly silent, and it seemed he had already passed away. The owner was grateful for his virtue and worshiped him devoutly. He used the money to run his business for several years, becoming wealthy in the entire village, but now he is gradually declining.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 得貴人發恻隐心 - Literally 'to gain a noble person's compassionate heart,' meaning to receive the sympathy or kindness of a powerful or influential individual.
  • 调饥甫慰 - Literally 'to soothe hunger just now,' but here it means to alleviate a pressing need or hardship.
  • 以蛹之以茧自缚 - A metaphorical expression meaning to bind oneself like a silkworm in its cocoon, symbolizing self-imposed limitations or entrapment.
  • 缄口制心 - Literally 'to seal one's mouth and control one's heart,' meaning to restrain one's speech and emotions.
  • 隐憾 - Literally 'hidden resentment,' but here it refers to underlying dissatisfaction or regret.
  • 奋拯溺扶危之志 - Literally 'to vigorously rescue the drowning and support the endangered,' meaning to have a strong desire to help those in distress.
  • 遏偎红倚翠之心 - Literally 'to curb the heart that clings to red and leans on green,' a metaphor for resisting sensual or material temptations.
  • 孟浪之谈 - Literally 'reckless talk,' referring to hasty or irresponsible speech.
  • 苟且之事 - Literally 'careless or improper actions,' referring to actions taken without proper consideration or principle.
  • 背明德而逞私欲 - Literally 'to abandon bright virtue and indulge in private desires,' meaning to act against moral principles for personal gain.
  • 以义始而以利终 - Literally 'to start with righteousness but end with profit,' meaning to begin with noble intentions but end up motivated by self-interest.
  • 鲰生翘首跂足之所望于贵人者哉 - Literally 'the small man's hope for the noble person,' referring to the humble person's expectations from a powerful individual.
  • 理痼于中，言激于外 - Literally 'principles are rooted inside, words are provoked from outside,' meaning that inner principles are strong but may be provoked by external circumstances.
  • 宥其冒昧 - Literally 'to pardon its rashness,' meaning to forgive its boldness or lack of caution.
  • 取共憨愚 - Literally 'to take its foolishness,' meaning to accept its naivety or simplicity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贵人 - Refers to a noble or influential person, often in a feudal or hierarchical context.
  • 恻隐心 - Refers to compassion or mercy, a key virtue in Confucian ethics.
  • 奋拯溺扶危之志 - Reflects the Confucian ideal of actively helping those in need.
  • 遏偎红倚翠之心 - A metaphor for resisting sensual temptations, often associated with Buddhist or Taoist teachings.
  • 孟浪之谈 - Refers to reckless or irresponsible speech, a concept found in classical Chinese moral philosophy.
  • 背明德而逞私欲 - Reflects the Confucian emphasis on moral integrity and the dangers of succumbing to personal desires.
  • 以义始而以利终 - Reflects the Confucian critique of actions that start with noble intentions but end in self-interest.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The complex sentence structure must be read as a single thought, where the subject is bound by both external pressures and internal limitations.
  • This clause describes the subject's self-imposed limitations and the resulting internal dissatisfaction.
  • This clause expresses the hope for virtuous individuals to act with compassion and resist temptations.
  • This clause warns against acting recklessly and succumbing to personal desires, leading to a loss of moral integrity.
  • This rhetorical question emphasizes the discrepancy between the humble person's expectations and the reality of the situation.
  • This clause contrasts inner principles with external provocation.
  • This closing clause expresses a plea for forgiveness and acceptance of the subject's naivety.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '霞固开元人' is straightforward, but '固' can imply 'indeed' or 'certainly,' adding emphasis.
  • The sentence structure '耿为总兵时，尝统兵过宁远' is clear, but '尝' indicates a past event, and '过' suggests movement through a place.
  • The parallel structure '牧豕于田畔，一老妪坐户下缉苎麻' describes two separate actions, but they are linked by the setting.
  • The contrast between simplicity and beauty in '虽乱头粗服，脂粉不施，而眉目如画' is clear, but '虽...而...' emphasizes the unexpected beauty.
  • The forceful action '掠之以归' is clear, but '以归' suggests a purposeful taking.
  • The parallel structure '修短得衷，纤秾合度' describes her proportions, but '得衷' and '合度' emphasize balance.
  • The parallel structure '玉肌花貌，艳丽殊常' describes her beauty, but '殊常' emphasizes its extraordinariness.
  • The contrast between desire and opposition in '屡欲纳之，而袁姬不容' is clear, but '而' emphasizes the opposition.
  • The suddenness of the event in '忽归林' is emphasized by '忽.'
  • The duration of her stay in '倩霞在耿府十年' is clear, but '十年' emphasizes the length of time.
  • The parallel structure '府中事无巨细，悉能言之' emphasizes her comprehensive knowledge.
  • The setting and activity in '姨及诸女眷，逐日于绣窗茶榻间听其追术' are clear, but '逐日' emphasizes the regularity.
  • The purpose of her storytelling in '以广新闻' is clear, but '以' emphasizes the intent.
  • The comparison in '比诸媚猪艾豭之条' is clear, but '比诸' emphasizes the analogy.
  • The characterization of the events in '为逆藩秽史' is clear, but '为' emphasizes the classification.

  • 此吾主报国之时，即吾侪报主之时也 - A statement expressing that this is the time for the speaker's master to serve the country and for the speaker and his companions to serve their master.
  • 仆愿往 - '仆' is a humble self-reference, and '愿往' means 'I am willing to go.'
  • 市马造车，制穹庐，备粮糗以从 - '市马造车' means 'to buy horses and make carriages,' '制穹庐' means 'to build a tent,' and '备粮糗以从' means 'to prepare food and provisions to follow.'
  • 谢乃喟然叹曰 - '谢' is a name, '乃' means 'then' or 'thereupon,' '喟然叹曰' means 'he sighed deeply and said.'
  • 吾向以为黠者有用，朴者可用也 - '吾向以为' means 'I previously thought,' '黠者有用' means 'the cunning ones are useful,' and '朴者可用也' means 'the simple ones are usable.'
  • 今而知黠者有用而不可用，而戆者可用也；朴者可用而实无用，而戆者有用也 - '今而知' means 'now I know,' '黠者有用而不可用' means 'the cunning ones are useful but not usable,' '而戆者可用也' means 'but the straightforward ones are usable,' '朴者可用而实无用' means 'the simple ones are usable but actually useless,' and '而戆者有用也' means 'but the straightforward ones are useful.'
  • 遂养以为子，名戆子焉 - '遂养以为子' means 'he then adopted him as a son,' and '名戆子焉' means 'and named him Gāngzi.'
  • 至军营，居未久，而资斧告匮 - '至军营' means 'to the military camp,' '居未久' means 'did not stay long,' '而资斧告匮' means 'and soon ran out of funds' (where '资斧' refers to money or resources).
  • 鬻及裘、马 - '鬻及' means 'sold even,' '裘' refers to furs or coats, and '马' refers to horses.
  • 戆子日荷火枪，出十余里外，猎取麋鹿獾兔 - '戆子日荷火枪' means 'Gāngzi carried a fire gun every day,' '出十余里外' means 'went out more than ten miles,' and '猎取麋鹿獾兔' means 'hunted deer, badgers, and rabbits.'
  • 以谢谋餐 - '以谢谋餐' means 'to provide meals for the master.'
  • 蹶而仆 - '蹶而仆' means 'stumbled and fell.'
  • 足陷入地中尺余 - '足陷入地中尺余' means 'his foot sank into the ground by more than a foot.'
  • 出足视，沙中白金灿然 - '出足视' means 'he pulled out his foot and looked,' and '沙中白金灿然' means 'and saw shining white gold in the sand.'
  • 数之，得二十巨铤，适千金 - '数之' means 'counted it,' '得二十巨铤' means 'and got twenty large bars,' and '适千金' means 'which totaled a thousand gold coins.'
  • 取之以归，谢以咨白将军 - '取之以归' means 'took it back,' and '谢以咨白将军' means 'the master consulted General Bái.'
  • 将军闻而异之，询其故，得知戆子所为 - '将军闻而异之' means 'General Bái was surprised to hear about it,' '询其故' means 'asked about the reason,' and '得知戆子所为' means 'and learned that it was Gāngzi's doing.'
  • 拊髀曰 - '拊髀曰' means 'patted his thigh and said.'
  • 沙漠乌得有藏金，盖天所以旌义仆也 - '沙漠乌得有藏金' means 'How could there be hidden gold in the desert?' and '盖天所以旌义仆也' means 'It must be that heaven is rewarding a loyal servant.'
  • 仍以金归谢，召戆子，奖以衣、裘、羊、马、金十两 - '仍以金归谢' means 'still returned the gold to the master,' '召戆子' means 'called for Gāngzi,' and '奖以衣、裘、羊、马、金十两' means 'and rewarded him with clothes, furs, sheep, horses, and ten gold pieces.'

  • 戆子劝其勇退，谢致仕颐养林泉 - '戆子劝其勇退' means 'Gāngzi advised him to retire bravely,' and '谢致仕颐养林泉' means 'the master retired and enjoyed his later years in seclusion.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 素談經濟 - 通常涉及經濟事務；指此人經常參與經濟討論。
  • 鞠躬盡瘁 - 古典成語，意為竭盡全力服務。
  • 夙夜酬知 - 日夜辛勞以履行職責和報答恩情。
  • 請命出巡 - 請求批准進行巡視。
  • 審其山川形势 - 檢查地形和地貌。
  • 親繪地图 - 親自繪製地圖。
  • 西负连山，东滨巨海 - 描述地區的地理特徵。
  • 长林大泽，广斥无边 - 描述自然環境。
  • 四境分为大镇，置使相，各辖六七城，皆宗臣领之 - 描述行政結構。
  • 向南八百里有都会，名大石国 - 描述一個名為大石國的主要城市。
  • 其风慓悍，乐于战斗 - 描述人民的風俗和氣質。
  • 国人畏之 - 描述人民對該主要城市的恐懼。
  • 悉了然于胸中 - 完全理解於心中。
  • 归国复命 - 返回國家並報告。
  • 退而上疏 - 提交奏折。
  • 奉命巡视封疆 - 接受命令巡視邊境。
  • 往返数千里，经历四十城 - 描述巡視的範圍。
  • 郡县无秦之酷吏，边关皆汉之嫖姚 - 描述沒有殘酷的官吏，邊關有漢朝的勇猛將士。
  • 三老兴歌，万民安堵 - 描述人民的和平與和諧狀態。
  • 太平有象 - 有和平的跡象。
  • 如此可徵 - 可以這樣驗證。
  • 古之圣君贤相 - 古代的聖君賢相。
  • 安不忘危 - 在和平時期不忘危險。
  • 治必防乱 - 治理必須防止混亂。

  • 三老兴歌，万民安堵 - 描述人民的和平與和諧狀態。

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 竭民力以纵口腹之欲 - Using people's labor to satisfy the king's personal desires.
  • 非盛德事 - Such actions are not virtuous.
  • 学贵流通而忌拘泥 - Learning should be flexible and open, but rigid and narrow-minded.
  • 戾人情而乖时务 - Actions that go against human nature and current affairs.
  • 王安石所以非救时宰相也 - Wang Anshi was not a good prime minister for his time.
  • 国人情风土 - The customs and local conditions of the country.
  • 高原硗瘠，下隰斥卤 - The land is barren and salty.
  • 民不耕而食，赖游猎以为生计 - The people do not farm but rely on hunting and fishing for their livelihood.
  • 巨鱼出水，是天赐丰年 - The appearance of the fish is a sign of a bountiful year.
  • 见山童水远，深以为不可 - Xiu found the mountain barren and the water far away, deeming it unsuitable for the new capital.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 海滨 - Refers to the coastal area.
  • 巨鱼 - A large fish, possibly a metaphor for something significant or unusual.
  • 潮落失水 - A natural phenomenon where the tide recedes, leaving the fish stranded.
  • 王命贵臣玄蚼 - The king's command to a high-ranking official named Xuan Fu.
  • 全国之民 - The entire population of the country.
  • 修 - The official named Xiu, who submitted a memorial to advise against the king's order.
  • 王安石 - A famous statesman during the Song Dynasty, known for his reforms.
  • 相国 - The prime minister, who has governed for many years.
  • 太史 - The historian, who reported the impending flood.
  • 镇北都护宁朔侯 - A high-ranking military official and member of the court.
  • 积石山 - A high, distant, and deep mountain, suitable for building a new capital.

Proper Nouns

  • 王 - The king.
  • 贵臣玄蚼 - The high-ranking official named Xuan Fu.
  • 修 - The official named Xiu.
  • 王安石 - A famous statesman during the Song Dynasty.
  • 相国 - The prime minister.
  • 太史玄□ - The historian named Xuan □.
  • 镇北都护宁朔侯侍中玄□ - The general and member of the court named Xuan □.
  • 积石山 - A high, distant, and deep mountain.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 封章 - Formal memorial presented to the emperor.
  • 周视名山 - To examine or survey famous mountains thoroughly.
  • 禹迹无存 - The traces of the great Yu (a legendary Chinese ruler) no longer exist.
  • 河源殊远 - The source of the river is extremely distant.
  • 乃疆乃理 - To demarcate boundaries and organize territories.
  • 非同亶父之岐 - Not the same as the fertile land of Da Pu (a mythical place).
  • 常邑常宁 - Ordinary towns and peaceful regions.
  • 讵等盘庚之亳 - How can it be compared to Pan Geng's Bo (a historical capital).
  • 窃思旧都巩固 - I humbly think that the old capital is firmly established.
  • 负山海以称雄 - Relying on mountains and seas to claim dominance.
  • 数代承平 - Several generations of peace and stability.
  • 通鱼盐以为利 - Trading fish and salt as a source of profit.
  • 不如绥爰有众 - It is better to settle the people.
  • 奠厥攸居 - To establish a place for them to live.
  • 黄门以事关重大 - The eunuch official thought the matter was of great importance.
  • 即时转奏 - Immediately forwarded the memorial.
  • 书生之见 - The perspective of a scholar.
  • 胡不远大如此 - Why so narrow-minded?
  • 伻来以图及献卜 - Messengers came with plans and divination results.
  • 知相国思深虑远 - Knowing that the Chancellor thinks deeply and far-sightedly.
  • 足见忠爱 - Clearly demonstrates loyalty and love.
  • 惟是都城滨海 - Only that the capital is by the sea.
  • 水患堪虞 - There is a risk of water disasters.
  • 如迁都之议一梗 - If the proposal to move the capital is blocked.
  • 则通国之民，皆鱼鳖矣 - Then the entire population would be like fish and turtles.
  • 兹更使中书令玄蚼为相国辅 - Now, the imperial command is to appoint the Secretary of the Central Secretariat, Xuan Ke, as the Chancellor's assistant.
  • 速定新都 - Quickly determine the new capital.
  • 寡人率宫嫔臣民，行当就迁矣 - I will lead the palace ladies, officials, and people to move soon.
  • 修得诏默然 - The Chancellor received the imperial decree silently.
  • 玄蚼至 - Xuan Ke arrived.
  • 乃相与于山之南麓 - They then worked together at the southern foot of the mountain.
  • 筑城垣 - Built city walls.
  • 建宫室 - Constructed palaces and buildings.
  • 开阛阓 - Opened markets.
  • 葺民居 - Renovated residential areas.
  • 连夜兴作 - Worked day and night.
  • 工粗竣 - The construction was roughly completed.
  • 王已徙都中十余万户 - The king had already moved over ten thousand households to the new capital.
  • 陆续而至 - Arrived in succession.
  • 修大惊 - The Chancellor was greatly surprised.
  • 遮道而谏曰 - Blocked the way and advised, saying.
  • 王奈何轻弃根本之地 - Why did you lightly abandon the fundamental land?
  • 独不虑敌国外患 - Did you not consider the threats from external enemies?
  • 乘间窃发耶 - Seizing an opportunity to launch an attack?
  • 王降舆慰劳 - The king descended from his carriage to comfort and encourage.
  • 改积石山曰定都山 - Changed the name of Mount Jishui to Dingdu Mountain.
  • 以修为故都留守 - Appointed the Chancellor as the governor of the old capital.
  • 晋爵定都公 - Elevated his title to Duke of Dingdu.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 禹迹 - Traces of the legendary ruler Yu, known for his flood control efforts.
  • 河源 - The source of the Yellow River, a symbol of national origin and strength.
  • 亶父之岐 - A mythical place associated with the early Zhou dynasty, known for its fertile land.
  • 盘庚之亳 - The capital of the Shang dynasty under King Pan Geng, a symbol of historical significance.
  • 书生之见 - The perspective of a scholar, often used to describe a narrow or impractical viewpoint.
  • 黄门 - A eunuch official in the imperial court.
  • 中书令 - The head of the Central Secretariat, a high-ranking official.
  • 玄蚼 - A historical figure, possibly a fictional character in this context.
  • 积石山 - A mountain, possibly a real location or a fictional one.
  • 定都山 - The newly named mountain, symbolizing the new capital.
  • 故都留守 - The governor of the old capital, responsible for maintaining order and administration.
  • 晋爵 - To elevate one's title or rank.
  • 定都公 - A noble title, indicating a high-ranking official.

Proper Nouns

  • 封章 - Formal memorial.
  • 禹 - Legendary ruler known for flood control.
  • 河源 - Source of the Yellow River.
  • 亶父之岐 - Mythical place associated with the early Zhou dynasty.

  • 晋爵 - Elevate one's title or rank.
  • 定都公 - Noble title, indicating a high-ranking official.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 拜命即行 - Formal expression meaning 'received the order and immediately set out,' indicating prompt obedience to a command.
  • 不预闻 - Means 'not informed in advance,' suggesting a lack of prior knowledge or involvement.
  • 从人半路多亡去 - Literally 'many followers deserted halfway,' indicating that a significant number of people abandoned the journey.
  • 殊深愤恚 - Means 'deeply resentful and angry,' expressing strong emotional distress.
  • 见万井一空 - Literally 'saw a thousand wells empty,' a metaphor for seeing widespread desolation or abandonment.
  • 孤臣恋恋 - Means 'a loyal minister who is deeply attached,' expressing a sense of loyalty and longing.
  • 解组归田 - Means 'to resign and return to the countryside,' indicating the desire to retire from public life.
  • 遂我初服 - Means 'to resume my original attire,' symbolizing a return to one's true self or former state.
  • 名利场中非复我侧足地也 - Literally 'the field of fame and profit is no longer a place where I can stand,' indicating that the pursuit of fame and profit is no longer suitable for the speaker.
  • 流言飞语 - Means 'rumors and slander,' referring to false or malicious gossip.
  • 未之深晰 - Means 'not thoroughly understood,' indicating a lack of clear insight.
  • 拘拘于不迁之议 - Means 'stuck in the argument against relocation,' suggesting an unwillingness to consider change.
  • 数百万生灵，尽葬鱼腹 - Literally 'millions of lives, all buried in the fish's belly,' a metaphor for widespread suffering or death.
  • 讵忍见之乎 - Means 'how could I bear to see it?' expressing a strong emotional reaction.
  • 请相国少留八日 - Means 'please, your highness, stay for eight more days,' a polite request for delay.
  • 当见灾异 - Means 'you will see disasters,' indicating a prediction of calamity.
  • 相国之怨，谅亦可少息矣 - Means 'the chancellor's grievances, I believe, will be somewhat alleviated,' suggesting a reduction in anger or dissatisfaction.
  • 停骖焉 - Means 'stopped the carriage,' indicating a decision to halt the journey.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 公主及诸子皆不预闻 - Refers to the princess and her children being unaware of the situation, indicating a lack of communication or involvement.
  • 万井一空 - A metaphor for widespread desolation, possibly referring to the aftermath of a disaster or war.
  • 孤臣恋恋 - A term used to describe a loyal minister who is deeply attached to his duties, often in a context of political turmoil or personal sacrifice.
  • 解组归田 - A common phrase in Chinese literature referring to the act of resigning from office and returning to a simple, rural life, often associated with retirement or withdrawal from public life.
  • 名利场中非复我侧足地也 - A reference to the pursuit of fame and profit, often seen as a corrupting influence in Chinese literature and philosophy.
  • 流言飞语 - A term for rumors and slander, often used to describe false or malicious gossip that can cause harm.
  • 拘拘于不迁之议 - Refers to the argument against relocation, possibly in the context of a political or military decision.
  • 数百万生灵，尽葬鱼腹 - A metaphor for widespread suffering or death, possibly referring to the consequences of a war or disaster.
  • 讵忍见之乎 - A rhetorical question expressing a strong emotional reaction, often used to convey deep distress or anger.
  • 请相国少留八日 - A polite request for delay, possibly made by a concerned individual or group.
  • 当见灾异 - A prediction of calamity, often used in the context of warnings or prophecies.
  • 相国之怨，谅亦可少息矣 - A statement suggesting that the chancellor's grievances will be somewhat alleviated, possibly through a change in circumstances or a resolution of a conflict.
  • 停骖焉 - A term for stopping the carriage, often used to indicate a decision to halt a journey or an action.

Proper Nouns

  • 修 - The name of the main character, likely a high-ranking official or chancellor.
  • 王 - Refers to the king or ruler.
  • 关吏 - Refers to the gatekeeper or official at the border.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 天地陰晦 - Literally 'heaven and earth dark and gloomy,' used to describe a severe storm or natural disaster.
  • 大雨如傾 - A vivid metaphor for heavy rain falling like a bucket being emptied.
  • 浃旬不止 - Refers to a prolonged period of rain, indicating a significant event.
  • 平地水深数丈 - Describes a severe flood, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 树头牵夫藻荇 - Describes the scene of trees submerged in water, a common natural phenomenon.
  • 舵尾压于楼台 - Suggests the overwhelming force of the water, a metaphor for the power of nature.
  • 骇目惊心 - Describes the terrifying nature of the scene, a common literary device.
  • 声如万马 - Emphasizes the intensity of the storm, a common literary device.
  • 关城地高，犹浸三版 - Indicates the extent of the flooding, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 修始信迁都之计非左 - Refers to the historical context of relocating the capital, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 乃西向再拜而泣曰 - A formal expression of remorse and submission, a common literary device.
  • 臣负王心矣 - Expresses deep regret, a common literary device.
  • 纵王不见谴 - A hypothetical concession, a common literary device.
  • 臣何面目复见臣民乎 - Expresses shame and self-reproach, a common literary device.
  • 言讫，掷仆头于地 - A dramatic gesture of despair, a common literary device.
  • 跃身赴水 - A decisive act of self-sacrifice, a common literary device.
  • 渹然一声 - Describes the sound of the leap, a common literary device.
  • 午梦忽觉 - Indicates the end of the dream, a common literary device.
  • 依然身卧桃笙 - Suggests the dreamer's return to reality, a common literary device.
  • 夏雨初歇 - Sets the scene for the narrator's awakening, a common literary device.
  • 檐溜犹滴 - Describes the lingering effects of the rain, a common literary device.
  • 蹶然而起 - Indicates a sudden action, a common literary device.
  • 自讼奇奇 - Expresses self-reproach, a common literary device.
  • 蹑履出户 - Describes a deliberate action, a common literary device.
  • 缘阶而行 - Indicates movement, a common literary device.
  • 冥想梦中去路 - Suggests reflection, a common literary device.
  • 依稀可认 - Describes a faint memory, a common literary device.
  • 寻踪至西墙下 - Indicates a search, a common literary device.
  • 花砖缺处 - Describes a specific detail, a common literary device.
  • 有小穴，大如钱 - Indicates a small opening, a common literary device.
  • 恍似东关形势 - Suggests a connection to the dream, a common literary device.
  • 对穴窥之 - Describes an action, a common literary device.
  • 则梅之渔沼假山历历可辨 - Then the fish pond, fake mountains, and other features of Mei's garden are clearly visible, indicating recognition.
  • 穴口有游蚁出入 - Ants are entering and exiting the hole, suggesting a natural detail.
  • 方悟四十年功名富贵，皆梦中蚁国幻化而为之也 - Only then does he realize that forty years of fame, wealth, and power were all illusions in a dream, a key moment of epiphany.
  • 闲斋曰 - Xianzhai says, introducing the narrator's commentary.
  • 梅慷慨，修耿介 - Mei is generous, and Xiu is upright, describing the characters' personalities.
  • 皆仙品也 - Both are like immortals, praising their character.
  • 故不学仙而入仙籍 - Therefore, they enter the ranks of immortals without learning the ways of immortals, suggesting a natural state of being.
  • 习见世人于忠孝根本之地及一切福田 - Frequently seeing people neglecting the foundations of loyalty and filial piety and all fields of merit, criticizing societal behavior.
  • 皆荒芜不治 - All are neglected and uncultivated, emphasizing the neglect.
  • 而功名富贵，声色货利之场，中热如烧 - But in the fields of fame, wealth, and sensual pleasures, they are burning with desire, contrasting neglect with obsession.
  • 及稍不如愿，辄妄欲学仙 - When they do not get their way, they rashly wish to become immortals, criticizing their superficial desires.
  • 其实七情六欲，触绪纷来 - In reality, their seven emotions and six desires arise in confusion, describing the complexity of human nature.
  • 虽有彭咸在侧，前推而后挽之 - Even if there were Peng Xian beside them, pushing them forward and pulling them back, suggesting the influence of moral guidance.
  • 恐一步行，万不敌其一步却也 - They would fear that one step forward is not worth a thousand steps back, expressing the difficulty of change.

[192]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 兰岩曰 - A common Classical Chinese phrase meaning 'Lan Yan said,' introducing a commentary or statement.
  • 四十年功名显赫，转成一梦耳 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'Forty years of fame and glory, which turned out to be just a dream.'
  • 拘迂见斥 - A term meaning 'criticism for being rigid and narrow-minded.'
  • 总不免书生之见 - A phrase meaning 'one cannot escape the views of a scholar.'
  • 修生不达时，修生乃悟道矣 - A statement meaning 'When a scholar does not achieve success, the scholar realizes the way.'
  • 来存予家老仆来存者 - A phrase meaning 'Lai Cun, an old servant of my family.'
  • 李姓，德名 - A phrase meaning 'Surname Li, given name De.'
  • 善国语 - A term meaning 'proficient in the national language.'
  • 性情直，有膂力 - A phrase meaning 'with a straightforward disposition and strong physique.'
  • 少壮时，曾负贩于蒙古诸部落 - A phrase meaning 'In his youth, he once traded with various Mongolian tribes.'
  • 其人情风土，知之甚悉 - A phrase meaning 'He knows their customs and local conditions very well.'
  • 所遭怪异，数见不鲜 - A phrase meaning 'The strange things he encountered were not uncommon.'
  • 予从家君扶祖榇自闽入都 - A phrase meaning 'I accompanied my father to carry my grandfather's coffin from Fujian to the capital.'
  • 于仲家浅泊舟三日 - A phrase meaning 'We docked our boat at Zhongjiaqian for three days.'
  • 候放闸 - A term meaning 'waiting for the sluice to be opened.'
  • 夏夜苦热 - A phrase meaning 'the summer night was extremely hot.'
  • 披襟坐船头，对月当风 - A phrase meaning 'unbuttoned his robe and sat at the front of the boat, facing the moon and the wind.'
  • 向李询塞外风景及所见闻 - A phrase meaning 'asked Li about the scenery and what he had seen and heard in the frontier regions.'
  • 其人骑兽，似鹿而非 - A phrase meaning 'the people ride animals that look like deer but are not.'
  • 有语言，无文字，亦无机械 - A phrase meaning 'they have language but no writing, and no machinery.'
  • 如游循蜚因提之世 - A phrase meaning 'like the age of Yu Xun Fei Yin Ti.'
  • 其俗无主客 - A phrase meaning 'their custom is without hosts and guests.'
  • 客至张幕，辄走乞烟食 - A phrase meaning 'when guests arrive, they set up a tent and go to beg for tobacco and food.'
  • 坐而眙脾脯醢齑，与之，乃去 - A phrase meaning 'they sit and look at the meat, pickles, and other foods, and when given, they leave.'
  • 客至其幕，径入啜且啖 - A phrase meaning 'when guests come to their tent, they directly enter and drink and eat.'
  • 夜宿毡炕前，主代牧，不偿 - A phrase meaning 'at night, they sleep in front of the felt kang, the host takes care of the animals, and does not charge.'
  • 予顾之曰 - A phrase meaning 'I looked at them and said.'
  • 视争畔构讼，析产阋墙 - A phrase meaning 'compared to those who quarrel over boundaries and lawsuits, divide property, and fight within the family.'
  • 行百里者必腰缠 - A phrase meaning 'those who travel a hundred miles must carry money.'
  • 惠一餐者有德色 - A phrase meaning 'those who receive a meal show a look of gratitude.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 兰岩 - A pseudonym or name of the author or commentator.
  • 四十年功名显赫 - Refers to a long period of fame and success.
  • 修生 - Refers to a scholar or a person engaged in learning.
  • 来存 - The name of an old servant.

  • 金中虚而外朗，起脶纹 - Describes a stone that is hollow inside but bright on the outside, with ridges or patterns on the surface resembling fingerprints (脶纹).
  • 皆马肝石所孕也 - '所孕' means 'produced' or 'generated,' indicating that the ridged patterns are formed by the '马肝石' (stones resembling liver).
  • 初剖之，癞 - '初剖之' means 'when first cut open,' and '癞' describes a rough or bumpy surface.
  • 日炙雨濯，风扫霜雪浸，剥落尽，则光璀璨矣 - Describes the process of weathering and polishing the stone, where '日炙雨濯' means 'sun-baked and rain-washed,' '风扫霜雪浸' means 'wind-blown and frost-swept,' and '剥落尽' means 'all the rough layers are worn away,' resulting in a '光璀璨' (shiny and brilliant) appearance.
  • 予矍然曰：“异哉！天之剥落之者，乃所以璀璨之也。” - '矍然' means 'startled' or 'astonished,' and '异哉' expresses surprise. The phrase '天之剥落之者，乃所以璀璨之也' is a philosophical reflection on how natural processes (weathering) lead to beauty (shining).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 戈壁 - Refers to the Gobi Desert, a large desert region in northern China and southern Mongolia. '瀚海' is a poetic or archaic name for the desert.
  • 奇石 - Refers to unusual or peculiar rocks, which were often collected and admired in Chinese culture for their aesthetic and symbolic value.
  • 马肝石 - A metaphorical term for stones with a dark red or brown color, resembling liver. Such stones were valued for their unique appearance and were often used in art or as decorative objects.
  • 金中虚而外朗 - Describes a stone that is hollow inside but bright on the outside, which could be a metaphor for something that appears simple but has depth.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '由喀尔喀至巴里坤' indicates a geographical journey but does not specify the route or the duration.
  • The phrase '似猿非猿，似猴非猴' is a descriptive metaphor but does not provide a clear classification of the animal.
  • The phrase '中国呼为人同，甘凉人呼为野人，番人呼为噶里' lists different names for the same animal but does not explain the cultural significance of each name.
  • The phrase '往往窥视穹庐，见人饮食，辄乞其余' describes the animal's behavior but does not specify the frequency or the context of the behavior.
  • The phrase '或窃取烟具、小刀之属' indicates the animal's actions but does not specify the motivation or the consequences.
  • The phrase '杀之不忍，逐之复来' describes the human response to the animal but does not specify the reasons for the response.
  • The phrase '治任将归，啾唧马前，捉衔捩镫，泪下如沈' describes the animal's behavior when the narrator prepares to leave but does not specify the emotional state of the narrator.
  • The phrase '可止矣，若再行数里，将迷途不得返矣' is a warning to the animal but does not specify the consequences of not heeding the warning.
  • The phrase '天地之间，人为贵，非五官之谓也' introduces a philosophical concept but does not provide a clear definition of what it means to be a human.
  • The phrase '先官而具曰五常，后官而合曰五典，傅官而行曰五事' links the five senses to moral and intellectual virtues but does not specify the exact relationship between the two.
  • The phrase '官而不事，又无典常，则人将兽之' warns against the consequences of lacking moral and intellectual virtues but does not provide a clear example of what this means in practice.
  • The phrase '三五偕而后为人' emphasizes the importance of combining the five senses with moral and intellectual virtues but does not specify how this is achieved.
  • The phrase '人也，可不慎欤' concludes the passage with a moral lesson but does not provide specific advice on how to live a virtuous life.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 无意坐香肆中闲话 - Sitting idly in a tea house without intention; sets the scene of the narrator's casual visit.
  • 衣冠济楚，仆从如云 - Dressed neatly, with a retinue as numerous as clouds; emphasizes the man's wealth and status.
  • 肆主接待甚恭 - The shop owner received him with great respect; highlights the man's high social standing.
  • 叙坐，各道姓名 - They sat down and introduced themselves; indicates a formal introduction.
  • 鞠躬曰 - Bowed and said; shows the man's polite and respectful demeanor.
  • 弟张太学也，世为鹾商 - I am Zhang, a scholar, my family has been in the salt trade for generations; introduces the man's background.
  • 豚儿某在庠 - My son is in school; refers to the man's son being a student.
  • 是即公郎耶？名下士也 - Is that your son? He is indeed a worthy scholar; the narrator's response, indicating his recognition of the son's academic merit.
  • 张大喜 - Zhang was greatly pleased; shows Zhang's reaction to the narrator's compliment.
  • 延至其家 - Invited him to his home; describes the hospitality extended by Zhang.
  • 登堂拜父 - Visited his father in the hall; indicates a formal meeting with Zhang's father.
  • 谛视之，其貌如所得画中翁，逼肖 - Examined him closely, his appearance was exactly like the old man in the painting; the narrator's realization of the resemblance.
  • 虽异之，犹不甚为意 - Although he was surprised, he did not pay much attention; the narrator's initial reaction to the resemblance.
  • 越旬余，张父死 - After about ten days, Zhang's father died; marks the passage of time and a significant event.
  • 求画师写真，数易人，无能有仿佛于万一者 - He sought a painter to create a portrait, but several painters failed to capture even a semblance of his appearance; highlights the difficulty in finding a suitable painter.
  • 备述得画之由 - Fully explained the origin of the painting; the narrator's detailed account of how he obtained the painting.
  • 此狐借我，欲厚赠公，以报德也 - This fox borrowed the painting from me, wanting to give you a generous gift in return for your kindness; the fox's explanation of its actions.
  • 乃取画，赠以千金 - He took the painting and gave me a thousand gold coins; describes the transaction.
  • 某始得携子归里 - I was finally able to take my son back to my hometown; the narrator's outcome.
  • 迄今犹素封也 - Until now, I am still a wealthy person; indicates the narrator's current status.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 香肆 - A tea house or a place where tea is served, often a gathering place for scholars and intellectuals.
  • 鹾商 - Salt merchants, a term referring to wealthy families involved in the salt trade, which was a major industry in ancient China.
  • 庠 - An ancient term for a school or academy, indicating the son's education.
  • 写真 - A portrait or a realistic depiction of a person.
  • 狐借我 - A reference to a fox borrowing something, which in Chinese folklore often involves supernatural elements.
  • 素封 - A term for a wealthy person, indicating a high social and economic status.

Proper Nouns

  • 张太学 - Zhang, a scholar.
  • 鹾商 - Salt merchants, a term referring to wealthy families involved in the salt trade.
  • 庠 - An ancient term for a school or academy.
  • 写真 - A portrait or a realistic depiction of a person.
  • 狐借我 - A reference to a fox borrowing something, which in Chinese folklore often involves supernatural elements.
  • 素封 - A term for a wealthy person, indicating a high social and economic status.
